preamble: The shift from a voiced velar stop [g] (or its fricative variant [ɣ]) to the voiceless labiodental fricative [f] is a complex process involving lenition, devoicing, and labialization. It typically occurs when the velar consonant is influenced by preceding rounded back vowels, causing the friction to migrate from the back of the throat to the lips.

examples.note: The voiced velar fricative (derived from a velar stop) devoiced and labialized into [f].
Similar shift where the velar friction shifted to labiodental friction.

sources: Baugh, A. C. & Cable, T. (2013). A History of the English Language (6th ed.). Routledge.
